anethole.title: Anethole
anethole.summary: Anethole is the defining sweet-spice aromatic in anise and fennel, and the key driver of classical absinthe's louche behavior.
anethole.sensory: Sweet licorice, anisic warmth, polished fennel sweetness.
biochemistry: Absorbed through the gut and processed predominantly in the liver through oxidative metabolism and conjugation.
Discussed in the literature for antimicrobial, spasmolytic, and anti-inflammatory activity, though dose and context matter.
Best understood in product terms as a high-impact aromatic that shapes flavor perception more than a primary human signaling molecule.

fenchone.title: Fenchone
fenchone.summary: Fenchone provides the dry, camphoraceous counterweight that keeps fennel and anise profiles from collapsing into candy.
fenchone.sensory: Dry herbal camphor, bitter fennel, cool mineral sharpness.
biochemistry: Like many monoterpenes, it is lipophilic and rapidly distributed after oral intake, then metabolized in the liver.
Its biological relevance is mostly discussed in the broader context of essential-oil activity rather than as a standalone endogenous mediator.
In formulation it is valuable because it introduces neural crispness to otherwise soft anisic sweetness.

thujone.title: Thujone
thujone.summary: Thujone is one of the best-known wormwood-associated terpenes and a major symbolic marker in absinthe discourse.
thujone.sensory: Dry green bitterness, resin, sage-like edge, austere lift.
biochemistry: At higher exposures thujone is discussed as a GABA-A receptor modulator, which helps explain historical toxicology interest.
It is hepatically metabolized and is not itself a nutritional or endogenous target compound.
Modern relevance in absinthe is more about aroma identity and compliance than mythology.

linalool.title: Linalool
linalool.summary: Linalool broadens harsh herbal structures into something more perfumed and breathable.
linalool.sensory: Floral citrus, lavender-like softness, polished lift.
biochemistry: Rapidly absorbed and metabolized through common hepatic terpene pathways.
Often discussed in sensory neuroscience because it can shift perceived calmness and floral brightness in inhalation contexts.
Literature frequently explores anxiolytic and anti-inflammatory potential, but context and route of exposure matter.

citral.title: Citral
citral.summary: Citral is the sharp lemon leaf cue behind melissa and lemon verbena styles.
citral.sensory: Lemon zest, green rind, bright volatile citrus.
biochemistry: Citral is metabolized as a reactive fragrance aldehyde and then conjugated through common detoxification pathways.
Frequently investigated for antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory effects in essential-oil research.
From a human-response standpoint it strongly shapes freshness perception and can make bitter systems feel cleaner and more alert.

chlorophylls.title: Chlorophylls
chlorophylls.summary: Chlorophylls matter less as aroma molecules than as the visual anchor for naturally colored verte styles.
chlorophylls.sensory: Visual rather than aromatic: deep herbal green, opaque botanical signal.
biochemistry: Chlorophylls are plant pigments, not human signaling molecules, and are broken down during digestion rather than used intact.
Human interest centers on antioxidant context, pigment derivatives, and food-color stability rather than receptor pharmacology.
In product design their power is symbolic: they tell the eye what the palate should expect.

alpha-pinene.title: Alpha-pinene
alpha-pinene.summary: Alpha-pinene creates crisp lift and makes bitter herbal formulas feel high and airy instead of muddy.
alpha-pinene.sensory: Pine needle, resin, dry forest top note.
biochemistry: Absorbed quickly by inhalation or ingestion and metabolized in the liver into oxygenated terpene derivatives.
Research often explores bronchodilatory, anti-inflammatory, and cognitive-support associations, though evidence varies by model.
In a beverage aroma matrix it reads as clarity and lift rather than sweetness.

limonene.title: Limonene
limonene.summary: Limonene adds a broad citrus glow that can sharpen structure without tasting like juice.
limonene.sensory: Orange peel, citrus oil, bright terpene bloom.
biochemistry: Widely metabolized through oxidation and conjugation after gastrointestinal absorption.
Frequently studied for gastric, anti-inflammatory, and chemopreventive associations in preclinical contexts.
Consumer perception often reads limonene as freshness, solvency, and uplift.

terpinen-4-ol.title: Terpinen-4-ol
terpinen-4-ol.summary: Terpinen-4-ol is a quieter structural terpene that helps hold herbal and citrus notes together.
terpinen-4-ol.sensory: Soft herb, tea-tree-like cleanliness, muted camphor warmth.
biochemistry: Handled through the same broad metabolism pathways that process many volatile terpene alcohols.
Most human-interest research centers on antimicrobial and skin-relevant bioactivity in essential oils.
Its formulation value is balance: it keeps bright top notes from feeling detached from the body.

rosmarinic-acid.title: Rosmarinic acid
rosmarinic-acid.summary: Rosmarinic acid is one of the major phenolics behind the cooling, herbal depth of lemon balm and related Lamiaceae herbs.
rosmarinic-acid.sensory: Not strongly aromatic on its own; contributes green, tannic-herbal depth.
biochemistry: Commonly studied for antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activity, especially in melissa and rosemary literature.
It is metabolized into smaller phenolic fragments and conjugates after ingestion.
Its importance in the site is as a bridge between botanical identity and broader inflammatory signaling discourse.

anthocyanins.title: Anthocyanins
anthocyanins.summary: Anthocyanins give rouge systems their jewel-like red-violet tone and respond visibly to acidity.
anthocyanins.sensory: Visual first: ruby, garnet, magenta cues; lightly tannic-fruit context.
biochemistry: These pigments are heavily studied for antioxidant signaling and vascular or inflammatory associations, though bioavailability is limited and complex.
They are transformed rapidly in the digestive tract and circulation into numerous metabolites rather than persisting intact.
For product design they are powerful because color change carries meaning before flavor arrives.

geraniol.title: Geraniol
geraniol.summary: Geraniol is one of the clearest floral cues in rose-forward systems and helps rouge stay perfumed instead of merely tart.
geraniol.sensory: Rose petal, lychee, sweet floral citrus.
biochemistry: Metabolized like many floral terpene alcohols through oxidation and conjugation pathways.
Literature often explores antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory activity alongside fragrance toxicology and sensory response.
In formulation it creates the illusion of softness and bloom even when the structure is quite dry.

ternatins.title: Ternatins
ternatins.summary: Ternatins are the signature pigments of butterfly pea flower and the engine behind Detour's chromatic theater.
ternatins.sensory: Primarily visual: saturated blue with pH-sensitive movement toward violet and purple.
biochemistry: Like other anthocyanin-derived pigments, ternatins are discussed mainly in the context of antioxidant chemistry and pigment stability.
Human digestion transforms them substantially, so their biochemistry is more about metabolites than intact pigment persistence.
Their design relevance lies in visible pH behavior, which lets formulation become performance.

verbascoside.title: Verbascoside
verbascoside.summary: Verbascoside is a useful marker for the denser phenolic side of lemon verbena-style herbs.
verbascoside.sensory: Tea-like phenolic depth, herbal bitterness, dried-leaf structure.
biochemistry: Often studied for antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and neuroprotective potential in plant-extract research.
It undergoes hydrolysis and downstream phenolic metabolism after ingestion.
In product terms it helps explain why some lemon herbs feel structural instead of merely citrusy.

pinocamphone.title: Pinocamphone
pinocamphone.summary: Pinocamphone contributes some of hyssop's sharper medicinal and resinous edge.
pinocamphone.sensory: Camphor, resin, medicinal herb, austere lift.
biochemistry: Discussed in safety literature because ketone-rich essential oils can show neuroactive behavior at higher exposures.
Metabolized hepatically, with most practical concern centered on exposure management rather than nutritional function.
In formulas it adds old-world medicinal tension.

citric-acid.title: Citric acid
citric-acid.summary: Citric acid is a direct route to brightness and helps red and blue botanical systems feel alive.
citric-acid.sensory: Sour snap, lifted fruit perception, cleaner finish.
biochemistry: Citric acid is familiar to human biochemistry because citrate is a core intermediate in the TCA cycle, though dietary citric acid is not a targeted signaling supplement by default.
In foods and drinks it matters physiologically through acidity, mineral binding, and salivary response.
Perceptually it changes everything: sourness reorganizes aroma and color expectations.
